rationale.R1.1: The section declares two mutually exclusive principal contributions: ¶1 names the dialogue-based decomposition protocol as 'the principal scientific advance,' while ¶5 reframes the work as 'best understood as the dataset and evaluation harness it makes available.' Reviewing requires identifying which framing is authoritative and flagging the contradiction.
rationale.R1.2: ¶2 introduces a central orchestrator that 'arbitrates conflicting proposals' and 'issues the final action token'; ¶3 explicitly states the agents communicate over 'a fully decentralized peer-to-peer channel with no central coordinator.' These are irreconcilable architectural claims within the same section, making logical consistency load-bearing.
rationale.R1.3: ¶1 asserts the protocol 'generalizes across embodiments and task families without any task-specific training'; the reported evaluation (¶3–4) covers exactly one platform (dual-arm Franka) and one task family (tabletop pick-and-place). The generalization claim is not supported by the evidence presented, making evidence–claim alignment load-bearing.
rationale.R1.4: RoboChat is reported to 'match' — not exceed — the monolithic baseline's success rate, with inspectability as the only differentiated advantage. Reviewing should assess whether this delta is sufficient to sustain the novelty framing in ¶1.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the review focus primarily on technical content (logical consistency, contribution framing, evidence–claim alignment), on writing and prose quality, or on both with equal weight?
